query: evidence linking specialty care to survival improvements in congestive heart failure

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: HF is a complex clinical syndrome with high morbidity and mortality, necessitating an interprofessional approach to optimize patient-centered care and outcomes. Physicians, including clinical cardiologists, interventionalists, electrophysiologists, HF specialists, and cardiac imaging specialists, collaborate to diagnose, manage, and treat HF using evidence-based medical and procedural interventions. Advanced practitioners, such as HF nurse practitioners, play a crucial role in ongoing patient assessment, medication titration, and patient education to improve adherence to guideline-directed therapy. Specialty-trained HF nurses are vital in educating patients on lifestyle modifications, symptom recognition, and weight management to prevent exacerbations and reduce hospital admissions. Physical therapists help improve functional capacity and promote safe activity levels, while HF-trained social workers and case managers assess patients in community settings or through home visits to facilitate adherence to care plans and address social determinants of health that may impact disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure) -- Treatment / Management -- Stage C Heart Failure Management
retrieved_docs.content: Interprofessional management is indicated for improving self-care and mortality of patients with HF.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective interprofessional communication and care coordination are essential to enhancing patient safety, team performance, and long-term outcomes in HF management. Structured communication strategies, such as interdisciplinary rounds and shared electronic health records, ensure seamless information exchange among clinicians. Clinical pharmacists work closely with physicians and nurses to review patient medication lists, optimize pharmacologic therapy, and prevent adverse drug-drug interactions, thereby improving medication safety. Collaboration between primary care clinicians and cardiologists helps ensure continuity of care, minimize adverse effects of medical therapy, and slow disease progression. A well-coordinated, team-based approach significantly improves the quality of life for HF patients by integrating medical, pharmacologic, and lifestyle interventions while reducing hospitalizations and mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Congestive Heart Failure and Pulmonary Edema -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Treating heart failure and acute decompensated heart failure is challenging despite the use of maximal evidence-based therapy based on the stage of heart failure. Given the limited effect of current treatment strategies on the progression of heart failure, it is important to identify ways to maximize patient outcomes and quality of care by the interprofessional team. Patients at potential risk for heart failure based on comorbidities or other identified risk factors should receive appropriate evidence-based preventative counseling and treatments. When applicable, the primary care clinician who may be the most involved in managing the patient's risk factors should consult other specialists, including cardiologists, endocrinologists, pharmacists, cardiology nurses, and nutritionists, to ensure they provide the best advice and treatment for their patients. Nurses monitor patients, provide education, and collaborate with clinicians and the healthcare team to improve outcomes. Pharmacists review medications, inform patients and their families about potential adverse events, and monitor compliance. Given the propensity of patients with heart failure to require recurrent admissions, often because of non heart failure-related conditions, the collaboration between inpatient and outpatient services can be of benefit in the continuity of care and help promote improved outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Heart failure is a leading cause of hospitalization and represents a significant clinical and economic burden. The long-term goal of treatment is to avoid exacerbation of HF and decrease hospital readmission rates. It needs an interprofessional approach involving patients, physicians, nurses, pharmacists, families, and caretakers. Those strategies include early identification of high-risk patients, patient education, improving medication and dietary compliance, assuring close follow-up, introducing end-of-care issues, and tele-home monitoring if available. Primary care and emergency department providers often are the first to make this diagnosis. Referral to cardiologists is often appropriate. Cardiology, medical/surgical, and critical care nurses administer treatment, provide education, monitor patients, and communicate with the rest of the team so that everyone on the healthcare team operates from the same data set. The managing clinician would do well to consult with a board-certified cardiology pharmacist when initiating pharmaceutical care in HF cases. Pharmacists also review medicines, check the dosages, detect drug-drug interactions, and stress to patients and their families the importance of compliance. In end-stage cases, hospice care and nurses can work with the patient and their family to provide comfort care. These interprofessional collaborations will optimize patient outcomes in HF cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Failure -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: an interprofessional approach is crucial in patients with advanced heart failure with an increased risk of readmissions. [16] [23] In a systematic review of 29 randomized controlled trials, an interprofessional approach decreased heart-failure related hospital admissions by 27% and all-cause mortality by around 25%. This approach was also cost-effective. Well-trained nurses are an integral part of this approach since heart failure clinics led by nurses and in coordination with the patients' providers were very successful in enhancing patient outcome. In addition to the availability of heart failure clinics, training personnel in heart failure management and patient education are cornerstones to a successful an interprofessional approach. Telephone follow up reduced hospital readmissions due to heart failure but not mortality. [level II]

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure With Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline emphasizes palliative and supportive care for all patients with heart failure, including those with HFpEF. Primary palliative care should begin early and address high-quality communication, advance care planning, home and case management assistance, and other needs. The complexity of HFpEF necessitates ongoing research and a comprehensive, team-based approach to optimize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure) -- Treatment / Management -- Stage D Heart Failure Management
retrieved_docs.content: The recommended treatment for stage D (advanced HF) includes: Referral to an HF specialist is indicated. It is reasonable to utilize inotropic support and device therapy in patients awaiting mechanical cardiac support or transplants. Inotropic support alone can be used in patients not eligible for a transplant or mechanical cardiac support. Mechanical cardiac support, eg, a durable left ventricle assist device (LVAD) or ECMO, can be beneficial as a bridge to transplant. For highly selected patients, cardiac transplant is indicated to improve survival and quality of life. Shared decision-making should determine the goals of care. This includes considering comorbid conditions, frailty, and socio-economic support. Palliative care should be offered as indicated after shared decision-making.

retrieved_docs.content: Patient education and social support are required for optimal management.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure) -- Treatment / Management -- Stage C Heart Failure Management
retrieved_docs.content: Screening patients for frailty, depression, low literacy, low social support, and resource and transport logistics during healthcare encounters is reasonable.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure) -- Treatment / Management -- Stage C Heart Failure Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccination against respiratory illnesses is effective in reducing mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the different types and stages of heart failure. Identify the etiologies, epidemiology, clinical symptoms, and signs of heart failure. Highlight the significance of diverse therapeutic approaches in managing heart failure, including non-medical, medical, and interventional therapies. Outline the importance of the interprofessional team to improve outcomes and enhance care delivery in patients affected by heart failure. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure With Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity reviews the epidemiology, etiology, pathophysiology, signs, symptoms, evaluation, evidence-based management, guideline-directed therapy, complications, and prognosis of HFpEF. This activity also highlights the vital role of the interprofessional healthcare team in reducing the burden of symptoms and improving the quality of life and health-related outcomes for patients with HFpEF.

retrieved_docs.title: Heart Failure With Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of HFpEF requires a collaborative effort among healthcare professionals, including physicians, advanced practice providers, nurses, pharmacists, and others, to enhance patient-centered care, improve outcomes, ensure patient safety, and optimize team performance. Given that heart failure represents a common endpoint for various disease processes, including ischemic heart disease secondary to coronary artery disease, addressing the underlying etiologies and risk factors is crucial.

retrieved_docs.content: Exercise training is effective in improving functional class and quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiomegaly -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Heart failure readmissions account for a majority of national healthcare expenditures. Support programs are operating nationwide to minimize the number of congestive heart failure-related hospitalizations and reduce the economic strain. These programs utilize a collaborative approach with many integrated disciplines and providers. Some inpatient strategies include guideline-based care, discharge support by nurses, patient education, medication reconciliation by the pharmacist, and early post-discharge follow-up. A board-certified cardiology pharmacist can assist clinicians in selecting medications for blood pressure control and other factors where medication can provide ancillary benefits. Cardiology-specialized nurses are also valuable interprofessional team members, coordinating activities between various specialists and other clinicians and providing patient counseling. Promising outpatient strategies include interprofessional team engagement in heart failure clinics and disease management programs that make in-person contact with patients and provide individualized education. [41] Cardiomegaly and heart failure require an interprofessional team approach that includes physicians and specialists, specialty-trained nurses, pharmacists, and therapists working collaboratively to make optimal therapeutic choices that lead to the best possible patient outcomes. This interprofessional approach requires open communication between all members of the care team and accurate, updated documentation to be effective.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2886
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac disorder, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, and cardiac catheterization are clinically interrelated. Cardiac disorder is clinically associated with coronary artery disease and with cardiac catheterization. Coronary artery disease is clinically associated with congestive heart failure and also with cardiac catheterization. Congestive heart failure is clinically associated with cardiac catheterization. These connections indicate that patients with a cardiac disorder often have underlying coronary artery disease and may require cardiac catheterization for diagnosis or treatment; likewise, coronary artery disease can progress to congestive heart failure, and both conditions frequently lead to the use of cardiac catheterization. Recognizing these clinical associations supports comprehensive cardiovascular assessment and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2097
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery atherosclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي), congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), and rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي). Conversely, cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery atherosclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي), indicating a bidirectional relationship. These interconnections highlight that patients with coronary artery atherosclerosis frequently present with or are at increased risk for other cardiac conditions—including cardiac disorders, congestive heart failure, and rheumatic heart disease—and that cardiac disorders may also reflect underlying coronary artery disease. Recognizing these linked pathologies is essential for comprehensive cardiovascular evaluation and integrated patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a surgical intervention that encompasses several specific procedures, including بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation), بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation), and بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation). These various levels of lower‑extremity amputation are clinically associated with فشل قلبي احتقاني (congestive heart failure), reflecting the close link between severe cardiac dysfunction and the risk of limb loss. Recognizing this relationship is important for comprehensive patient management, as individuals with congestive heart failure often require careful vascular assessment and multidisciplinary care to address both cardiac and peripheral complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2883
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a broad surgical category that includes specific procedures such as بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation) and بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation). These lower‑extremity amputations are clinically associated with فشل قلبي احتقاني (congestive heart failure), indicating that patients who undergo or require amputation of the lower limb often have, or are at increased risk for, this form of heart failure. Recognizing the link between lower‑extremity amputation and congestive heart failure is important for comprehensive patient assessment, multidisciplinary management, and optimizing outcomes for individuals affected by these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2016
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with a range of cardiac conditions and diagnostic procedures. It is linked to cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي), congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), and heart murmurs (نفخة قلبية), reflecting its broad impact on cardiac function and symptomatology. In addition, coronary artery disease is closely connected to the use of cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية) as a key investigative and therapeutic tool. Cardiac disorders and congestive heart failure are also individually associated with cardiac catheterization, underscoring the procedure’s central role in evaluating and managing these interrelated cardiovascular problems. Together, these associations highlight the intertwined nature of coronary artery disease, various cardiac pathologies, and the pivotal role of cardiac catheterization in patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2726
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب قلبي, تصلب الشريان التاجي, فشل قلبي احتقاني, التهاب النسيج الخلوي, and قسطرة قلبية are closely interconnected clinical entities. اضطراب قلبي is clinically associated with تصلب الشريان التاجي, indicating that structural abnormalities of the coronary arteries often coexist with or contribute to broader cardiac dysfunction. تصلب الشريان التاجي is further linked to فشل قلبي احتقاني, reflecting that progressive atherosclerotic narrowing of the coronary vessels can lead to impaired myocardial perfusion and subsequent congestive heart failure. قسطرة قلبية is repeatedly noted as a clinical association for اضطراب قلبي, تصلب الشريان التاجي, فشل قلبي احتقاني, and التهاب النسيج الخلوي, underscoring its central role as a diagnostic and interventional tool for evaluating and managing these conditions. The association of التهاب النسيج الخلوي with قسطرة قلبية suggests that inflammatory processes of the cardiac tissue are either assessed during catheter‑based procedures or may represent a complication requiring catheter‑based intervention. Together, these relationships highlight the overlapping pathophysiology of cardiac disorders, coronary artery disease, heart failure, and inflammatory cardiac conditions, and emphasize the pivotal role of قسطرة قلبية in their assessment and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery sclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي) and with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني). Both coronary artery sclerosis and cardiac disorders are frequently evaluated using cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية), which is also linked clinically to cellular tissue inflammation (التهاب النسيج الخلوي) and to congestive heart failure. Computed tomography coronary vessel imaging (تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب) is a diagnostic modality related to coronary artery sclerosis and is connected to coronary disease (مرض التاجي). These interrelationships highlight the central role of coronary artery disease in the pathophysiology of cardiac disorders and heart failure, and underscore the importance of imaging and invasive catheter‑based assessment in patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1780
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac function is conceptually linked to the heart‑rate metric (معدل ضربات القلب), indicating that assessments of cardiac performance are grounded in heart‑rate measurements. The interpretation of cardiac function is particularly relevant in the context of non‑hypertensive congestive heart failure, a condition whose clinical findings are localized to the heart chambers (srdeční komory). Likewise, intraventricular pacing—a therapeutic intervention—targets the same anatomical site, the heart chambers, underscoring the chambers’ central role both in diagnosing specific forms of heart failure and in delivering pacing therapy. This interconnected framework highlights how heart‑rate evaluation, disease interpretation, and procedural treatment converge on the cardiac chambers to inform comprehensive patient care.
